\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{index::doc}}


\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{xtremes} is a Python library for various utilities useful in Extreme Value Statistics. So far, you will find tools to fit more than one
order statistics for parameter estimation as well as a Bootstrap device.

\sphinxAtStartPar
It is created within the ClimXtreme project and will provide supplementary code and simulations for the papers yet to come.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Check out the {\hyperref[\detokenize{usage::doc}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{doc}{Usage}}}} section for further information, including
how to {\hyperref[\detokenize{usage:installation}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Installation}}}} the project.

\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Note:}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This project is under active and heavy development.
\end{sphinxadmonition}

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Usage}
\label{\detokenize{usage:usage}}\label{\detokenize{usage::doc}}

\section{Installation}
\label{\detokenize{usage:installation}}\label{\detokenize{usage:id1}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
To use xtremes, it is possible to install it via pip

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp+gpVirtualEnv}{(.venv)} \PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZdl{} }pip\PYG{+w}{ }install\PYG{+w}{ }xtremes
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{The modules}
\label{\detokenize{usage:the-modules}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
So far, three modules are implemented. The module \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{xtremes.miscellaneous}} contains basic functionalities,
whereas \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{xtremes.topt}} is specialized on the influence of higher order statistics for
Maximum Likelihood estimations. \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{xtremes.Bootstrap}} provides a suitable Bootstrap device
for block maxima.

\sphinxAtStartPar
For each module, there will be a tutorial and a subsection in the API reference.

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{Using the TimeSeries Class}
\label{\detokenize{notebooks/timeseries:Using-the-TimeSeries-Class}}\label{\detokenize{notebooks/timeseries::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The TimeSeries Class is used to generate realizations of specific time series and extract their blockmaxima

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[1]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n+nn}{xtremes} \PYG{k}{as} \PYG{n+nn}{xx}
\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n+nn}{xtremes}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{topt} \PYG{k}{as} \PYG{n+nn}{hos}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this section, we create a \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TimeSeries}} object \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TS}} using the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{xtremes.HigherOrderStatistics}} module. The time series is generated with the following parameters:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{n=1000}}: The length of the time series.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{distr='GEV'}}: The distribution type, which is the Generalized Extreme Value distribution.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{modelparams=(0.5, 0.1, 0.1)}}: The parameters for the GEV distribution.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
We then simulate 200 realizations of this time series using the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{simulate}} method.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Finally, we extract the block maxima from the simulated time series with a block size of 10 using the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_blockmaxima}} method:

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[2]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{TS} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{hos}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{TimeSeries}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{n}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1000}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{distr}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{GEV}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{modelparams}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.1}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.1}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{simulate}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rep}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{200}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}blockmaxima}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}size}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{10}\PYG{p}{)}

\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TS.plot(rep={[}1,2{]})}} method is used to plot specific realizations of the time series. In this case, the method will plot the first and second realizations of the time series \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TS}}. This is useful for visualizing the behavior of the time series over different realizations and understanding the variability in the data.

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[3]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{plot}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rep}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nboutput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
\hrule height -\fboxrule\relax
\vspace{\nbsphinxcodecellspacing}

\makeatletter\setbox\nbsphinxpromptbox\box\voidb@x\makeatother

\begin{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{output_area}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{}
\noindent\sphinxincludegraphics[width=842\sphinxpxdimen,height=545\sphinxpxdimen]{{notebooks_timeseries_5_0}.png}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TS.plot\_with\_blockmaxima()}} method is used to visualize the time series along with its block maxima. This method plots the original time series data and overlays the block maxima on the same plot. This is useful for identifying extreme values within the time series and understanding how these extremes are distributed over time.

\sphinxAtStartPar
By visualizing both the time series and its block maxima, one can gain insights into the behavior of extreme events and their frequency.

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[4]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{plot\PYGZus{}with\PYGZus{}blockmaxima}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nboutput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
\hrule height -\fboxrule\relax
\vspace{\nbsphinxcodecellspacing}

\makeatletter\setbox\nbsphinxpromptbox\box\voidb@x\makeatother

\begin{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{output_area}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{}
\noindent\sphinxincludegraphics[width=1298\sphinxpxdimen,height=776\sphinxpxdimen]{{notebooks_timeseries_7_0}.png}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TS.plot\_blockmaxima(rep=1)}} method is used to plot the block maxima of a specific realization of the time series. In this case, the method will plot the block maxima for the first realization (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{rep=1}}).

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[5]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{plot\PYGZus{}blockmaxima}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rep}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}

\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nboutput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
\hrule height -\fboxrule\relax
\vspace{\nbsphinxcodecellspacing}

\makeatletter\setbox\nbsphinxpromptbox\box\voidb@x\makeatother

\begin{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{output_area}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{}
\noindent\sphinxincludegraphics[width=842\sphinxpxdimen,height=545\sphinxpxdimen]{{notebooks_timeseries_9_0}.png}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TS.plot\_blockmaxima(rep=1, plot\_type='hist')}} method is used to plot the block maxima of a specific realization of the time series as a histogram. In this case, the method will plot the block maxima for the first realization (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{rep=1}}).

\sphinxAtStartPar
By setting the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{plot\_type}} parameter to \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{'hist'}}, the block maxima are visualized in the form of a histogram, which helps in understanding the distribution of the block maxima values. This is useful for analyzing the frequency and range of extreme values within the time series.

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[6]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{plot\PYGZus{}blockmaxima}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rep}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{plot\PYGZus{}type}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{hist}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nboutput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
\hrule height -\fboxrule\relax
\vspace{\nbsphinxcodecellspacing}

\makeatletter\setbox\nbsphinxpromptbox\box\voidb@x\makeatother

\begin{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{output_area}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{}
\noindent\sphinxincludegraphics[width=842\sphinxpxdimen,height=545\sphinxpxdimen]{{notebooks_timeseries_11_0}.png}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\end{sphinxuseclass}

\subsection{Maximum Likelihood Estimation on High Order Statistics}
\label{\detokenize{notebooks/estimators:Maximum-Likelihood-Estimation-on-High-Order-Statistics}}\label{\detokenize{notebooks/estimators::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this notebook, we perform an analysis of time series data using the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{xtremes}} library. We utilize various estimators and statistical methods provided by the library to analyze and visualize the data.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The following variables are used in this notebook:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{PWM}: A \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PWM\_estimators}} object used for Probability Weighted Moments estimation.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{MLE}: A \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ML\_estimators}} object used for Maximum Likelihood estimation.

\end{itemize}

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[1]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n+nn}{xtremes} \PYG{k}{as} \PYG{n+nn}{xx}
\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n+nn}{xtremes}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{topt} \PYG{k}{as} \PYG{n+nn}{hos}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[2]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{TS} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{hos}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{TimeSeries}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{n}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1000}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{modelparams}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.1}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.1}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{simulate}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rep}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{200}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}blockmaxima}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}size}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{10}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}

\subsubsection{Probability Weighted Moments (PWM) Estimation}
\label{\detokenize{notebooks/estimators:Probability-Weighted-Moments-(PWM)-Estimation}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this section, we perform Probability Weighted Moments (PWM) estimation on the time series data. The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PWM\_estimators}} object is used for this purpose. Below are the steps involved:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Initialization}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     PWM = hos.PWM\_estimators(TS)}} We initialize the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PWM\_estimators}} object with the time series data \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TS}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{PWM Estimation}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     PWM.get\_PWM\_estimation()}} This method computes the PWM estimates for the given time series data.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Statistics Calculation}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     PWM.get\_statistics(gamma\_true=0)}} This method calculates the statistics based on the PWM estimates. Here, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{gamma\_true}} is the true value of the shape parameter used for comparison.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Confidence Intervals}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     PWM.get\_CIs()}} This method computes the confidence intervals for the PWM estimates.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{View Statistics}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     PWM.statistics}} This attribute holds the computed statistics, which can be accessed for further analysis or visualization.

\end{enumerate}

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[3]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{PWM} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{hos}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{PWM\PYGZus{}estimators}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{PWM}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}PWM\PYGZus{}estimation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{PWM}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}statistics}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{gamma\PYGZus{}true}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{PWM}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}CIs}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{PWM}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{statistics}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nboutput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
{

\kern-\sphinxverbatimsmallskipamount\kern-\baselineskip
\kern+\FrameHeightAdjust\kern-\fboxrule
\vspace{\nbsphinxcodecellspacing}

\sphinxsetup{VerbatimColor={named}{white}}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{output_area}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{}


\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxout}[3]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\{'gamma\_mean': 0.4708851765999637,
 'gamma\_variance': 0.016709443122544267,
 'gamma\_bias': 0.02918788455230181,
 'gamma\_mse': 0.017561375727182766,
 'mu\_mean': 0.5376701999751607,
 'mu\_variance': 0.0016648680442634882,
 'sigma\_mean': 0.3197043973154132,
 'sigma\_variance': 0.0020985834642473842,
 'gamma\_CI': array([0.26048308, 0.7876679 ]),
 'mu\_CI': array([0.47036141, 0.63408314]),

\end{sphinxVerbatim}



\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Plotting the distribution of the optained estimators together with a symmetrical CI is as easy as:

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[4]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{PWM}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{plot}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nboutput}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nblast}
\hrule height -\fboxrule\relax
\vspace{\nbsphinxcodecellspacing}

\makeatletter\setbox\nbsphinxpromptbox\box\voidb@x\makeatother

\begin{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{output_area}
\begin{sphinxuseclass}{}
\noindent\sphinxincludegraphics[width=842\sphinxpxdimen,height=545\sphinxpxdimen]{{notebooks_estimators_6_0}.png}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{nbsphinxfancyoutput}

\end{sphinxuseclass}
\end{sphinxuseclass}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The same works analogously for MLE. We use the PWM estimators as an initial guess for otimization. Instead of symmetrical CIs (default) we can also plot CIs with minimal width

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[5]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{MLE} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{hos}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{ML\PYGZus{}estimators}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{MLE}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}ML\PYGZus{}estimation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{PWM\PYGZus{}estimators}\PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PWM}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{MLE}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}statistics}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{gamma\PYGZus{}true}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{0}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{MLE}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}CIs}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{method}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{minimal\PYGZus{}width}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{MLE}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{plot}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\subsubsection{Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) with Frechet Distribution}
\label{\detokenize{notebooks/estimators:Maximum-Likelihood-Estimation-(MLE)-with-Frechet-Distribution}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this section, we perform Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) using the Frechet distribution on the time series data. The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Frechet\_ML\_estimators}} object is used for this purpose. Below are the steps involved:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Initialization}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     MLE = hos.Frechet\_ML\_estimators(TS)}} We initialize the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Frechet\_ML\_estimators}} object with the time series data \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TS}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{MLE Estimation}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     MLE.get\_ML\_estimation(PWM\_estimators= PWM)}} This method computes the MLE estimates for the given time series data using the PWM estimators as initial guesses for optimization.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Statistics Calculation}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     MLE.get\_statistics(alpha\_true=0)}} This method calculates the statistics based on the MLE estimates. Here, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{alpha\_true}} is the true value of the shape parameter used for comparison.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Confidence Intervals}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     MLE.get\_CIs()}} This method computes the confidence intervals for the MLE estimates.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{View Statistics}: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python     MLE.plot()}} This method plots the distribution of the obtained estimators together with the confidence intervals.

\end{enumerate}


\paragraph{Difference between Frechet and GEV Distributions}
\label{\detokenize{notebooks/estimators:Difference-between-Frechet-and-GEV-Distributions}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The key difference between using the Frechet distribution and the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution lies in the type of tail behavior they model:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Frechet Distribution}: This distribution is used to model data with heavy tails. It is a special case of the GEV distribution with a positive shape parameter. The Frechet distribution is particularly useful for modeling extreme values that follow a power\sphinxhyphen{}law decay.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{GEV Distribution}: The GEV distribution is a more general form that encompasses three types of distributions based on the shape parameter: Gumbel (light tails), Frechet (heavy tails), and Weibull (bounded tails). The GEV distribution provides more flexibility in modeling different types of extreme value behavior.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
By using the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Frechet\_ML\_estimators}}, we specifically focus on modeling heavy\sphinxhyphen{}tailed data, which is suitable for certain types of extreme value analysis.

\begin{sphinxuseclass}{nbinput}
{
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\llap{\color{nbsphinxin}[6]:\,\hspace{\fboxrule}\hspace{\fboxsep}}\PYG{n}{MLE} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{hos}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{Frechet\PYGZus{}ML\PYGZus{}estimators}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{TS}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{MLE}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}ML\PYGZus{}estimation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{PWM\PYGZus{}estimators}\PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PWM}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{MLE}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}statistics}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{alpha\PYGZus{}true}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{0}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{MLE}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}CIs}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{MLE}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{plot}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
}

\end{sphinxuseclass}

\chapter{Time Series and Extreme Value Analysis}
\label{\detokenize{topt:time-series-and-extreme-value-analysis}}\label{\detokenize{topt::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this tutorial, we will explore how to use the functionalities provided in \sphinxtitleref{topt.py} to simulate time series data, extract block maxima, and perform Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for extreme value distributions. We will also see how to work with real data to extract high\sphinxhyphen{}order statistics and compute MLE.

\sphinxAtStartPar
We will cover:
\sphinxhyphen{} Simulating time series data using the \sphinxtitleref{TimeSeries} class.
\sphinxhyphen{} Extracting block maxima and high\sphinxhyphen{}order statistics from the simulated data.
\sphinxhyphen{} Estimating parameters for GEV and Frechet distributions using PWM and MLE.
\sphinxhyphen{} Analyzing real\sphinxhyphen{}world datasets for extreme value statistics.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Let’s walk through each of these steps with code examples.


\section{Step 1: Simulating Time Series Data}
\label{\detokenize{topt:step-1-simulating-time-series-data}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The first step is to simulate a time series dataset using the \sphinxtitleref{TimeSeries} class. You can specify the length of the series, the type of distribution (e.g., GEV), and whether to apply any correlation structure (e.g., IID or ARMAX).

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how you can simulate a GEV\sphinxhyphen{}distributed time series:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{topt} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{TimeSeries}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Create a time series object with 100 data points, GEV distribution, and IID correlation}
\PYG{n}{ts} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{TimeSeries}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{n}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{distr}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{GEV}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{correlation}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{IID}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{modelparams}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Simulate the time series with 10 repetitions}
\PYG{n}{ts}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{simulate}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rep}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{10}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Print the simulated time series data}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ts}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{values}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Step 2: Extracting Block Maxima}
\label{\detokenize{topt:step-2-extracting-block-maxima}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Once the time series is generated, the next step is to extract block maxima from the data. Block maxima are the largest values within blocks of a certain size in the time series.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to extract block maxima with a block size of 5:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Extract block maxima with a block size of 5}
\PYG{n}{ts}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}blockmaxima}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}size}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Print the extracted block maxima}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ts}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{blockmaxima}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
In this example, the \sphinxtitleref{get\_blockmaxima()} function divides the time series into blocks of size 5 and extracts the maximum value from each block. You can adjust the stride (e.g., ‘DBM’ for disjoint blocks or ‘SBM’ for sliding blocks).


\section{Step 3: Extracting High Order Statistics}
\label{\detokenize{topt:step-3-extracting-high-order-statistics}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
High\sphinxhyphen{}order statistics refer to the second, third, or higher\sphinxhyphen{}largest values within a block of data. You can extract these using the \sphinxtitleref{get\_HOS()} method.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to extract the top 2 largest values from each block:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Extract the two highest values from each block}
\PYG{n}{ts}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}HOS}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{orderstats}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}size}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Print the high\PYGZhy{}order statistics}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ts}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{high\PYGZus{}order\PYGZus{}stats}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Step 4: Estimating Parameters with PWM}
\label{\detokenize{topt:step-4-estimating-parameters-with-pwm}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Once block maxima are extracted, you can estimate the parameters of the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution using Probability Weighted Moments (PWM). The \sphinxtitleref{PWM\_estimators} class handles this.

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{topt} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{PWM\PYGZus{}estimators}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Initialize PWM estimator with the time series data}
\PYG{n}{pwm} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PWM\PYGZus{}estimators}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ts}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Compute PWM estimators for GEV parameters}
\PYG{n}{pwm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}PWM\PYGZus{}estimation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Print the GEV parameter estimates}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{pwm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{values}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Step 5: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)}
\label{\detokenize{topt:step-5-maximum-likelihood-estimation-mle}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
To estimate the GEV or Frechet parameters using MLE, you can use the \sphinxtitleref{ML\_estimators} class. This method fits the distribution to the block maxima or high\sphinxhyphen{}order statistics.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to perform MLE for the GEV distribution:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{topt} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{ML\PYGZus{}estimators}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Initialize MLE estimator with the time series data}
\PYG{n}{ml} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{ML\PYGZus{}estimators}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ts}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Perform MLE for the GEV distribution}
\PYG{n}{ml}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}ML\PYGZus{}estimation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Print the MLE results}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{ml}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{values}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Step 6: Analyzing Real Data}
\label{\detokenize{topt:step-6-analyzing-real-data}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
You can also work with real\sphinxhyphen{}world datasets using the \sphinxtitleref{Data} class. This class allows you to extract block maxima and high\sphinxhyphen{}order statistics, and perform MLE on the dataset.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to analyze a real dataset:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{topt} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{Data}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Initialize the Data class with a real dataset}
\PYG{n}{data} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{Data}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mf}{2.5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{3.1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{1.7}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{4.6}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{5.3}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{2.2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{6.0}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Extract block maxima}
\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}blockmaxima}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}size}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Extract high\PYGZhy{}order statistics}
\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}HOS}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{orderstats}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}size}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Perform MLE on the dataset}
\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}ML\PYGZus{}estimation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{FrechetOrGEV}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{GEV}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Print the MLE results}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{ML\PYGZus{}estimators}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{values}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Conclusion}
\label{\detokenize{topt:conclusion}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this tutorial, we explored how to simulate time series data, extract block maxima and high\sphinxhyphen{}order statistics, and perform MLE for extreme value distributions. We also saw how to analyze real\sphinxhyphen{}world data for extreme value statistics using block maxima and MLE.

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Bootstrap and MLE Tutorial}
\label{\detokenize{bootstrap:bootstrap-and-mle-tutorial}}\label{\detokenize{bootstrap::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this tutorial, we will explore how to work with block maxima extraction, bootstrap resampling, and Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for extreme value distributions using the \sphinxtitleref{bootstrap.py} code.

\sphinxAtStartPar
We will cover:
\sphinxhyphen{} Block maxima extraction using the \sphinxtitleref{circmax()} function.
\sphinxhyphen{} Generating bootstrap samples with \sphinxtitleref{Bootstrap()}.
\sphinxhyphen{} Aggregating bootstrap samples and estimating Fréchet and GEV parameters using \sphinxtitleref{ML\_Estimator}.
\sphinxhyphen{} Running full bootstrapping procedures for MLE with \sphinxtitleref{FullBootstrap}.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Let’s walk through each of these steps with code examples.


\section{Step 1: Block Maxima Extraction}
\label{\detokenize{bootstrap:step-1-block-maxima-extraction}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The first step in working with extreme value statistics is often to extract block maxima from a dataset. The \sphinxtitleref{circmax()} function allows you to do this using either disjoint blocks or sliding blocks.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how you can extract block maxima from a sample:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n+nn}{numpy} \PYG{k}{as} \PYG{n+nn}{np}
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{bootstrap} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{circmax}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example dataset}
\PYG{n}{sample} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{array}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{4}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{6}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{7}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{8}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{9}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{10}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Extract disjoint block maxima (DBM)}
\PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}maxima\PYGZus{}dbm} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{circmax}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{sample}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Disjoint Block Maxima (DBM):}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}maxima\PYGZus{}dbm}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Extract sliding block maxima (SBM)}
\PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}maxima\PYGZus{}sbm} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{circmax}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{sample}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{SBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Sliding Block Maxima (SBM):}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}maxima\PYGZus{}sbm}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
As you can see, \sphinxtitleref{circmax()} allows you to specify the block size (\sphinxtitleref{bs}) and the stride method (\sphinxtitleref{DBM} or \sphinxtitleref{SBM}). DBM extracts maxima from non\sphinxhyphen{}overlapping blocks, while SBM uses overlapping blocks, providing more block maxima.


\section{Step 2: Bootstrapping a Sample}
\label{\detokenize{bootstrap:step-2-bootstrapping-a-sample}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Next, we’ll generate a bootstrap sample. Bootstrapping is a resampling technique used to estimate the variability of a statistic by randomly resampling the data with replacement.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how you can generate a bootstrap sample from the block maxima we extracted earlier:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{bootstrap} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{Bootstrap}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Generate a bootstrap sample from the block maxima}
\PYG{n}{boot\PYGZus{}sample} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{Bootstrap}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{block\PYGZus{}maxima\PYGZus{}dbm}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Bootstrap Sample:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{boot\PYGZus{}sample}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
In this case, the \sphinxtitleref{Bootstrap()} function takes a list or array as input and returns a new sample of the same size, created by randomly selecting elements from the original data with replacement.


\section{Step 3: Aggregating the Bootstrap Sample}
\label{\detokenize{bootstrap:step-3-aggregating-the-bootstrap-sample}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Once you have a bootstrap sample, the next step is to aggregate the counts of unique values in the sample. This aggregation helps us prepare the data for MLE by summarizing the frequencies of the unique values.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how you can aggregate the bootstrap sample:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{bootstrap} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{aggregate\PYGZus{}boot}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Aggregate the counts of unique values in the bootstrap sample}
\PYG{n}{aggregated\PYGZus{}sample} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{aggregate\PYGZus{}boot}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{boot\PYGZus{}sample}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Aggregated Bootstrap Sample:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{aggregated\PYGZus{}sample}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Now, we have an aggregated sample that shows the unique values and their corresponding counts. This aggregated data will be used in the next step for MLE.


\section{Step 4: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)}
\label{\detokenize{bootstrap:step-4-maximum-likelihood-estimation-mle}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxtitleref{ML\_Estimator} class allows us to perform MLE for either the Fréchet or GEV distribution using the aggregated bootstrap sample.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Let’s first perform MLE for the Fréchet distribution:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{bootstrap} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{ML\PYGZus{}Estimator}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Initialize the ML\PYGZus{}Estimator with the aggregated bootstrap sample}
\PYG{n}{estimator} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{ML\PYGZus{}Estimator}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{aggregated\PYGZus{}sample}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Perform MLE for the Fréchet distribution}
\PYG{n}{frechet\PYGZus{}params} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{estimator}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{maximize\PYGZus{}frechet}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Estimated Fréchet Parameters:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{frechet\PYGZus{}params}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Similarly, you can perform MLE for the GEV distribution:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Perform MLE for the GEV distribution}
\PYG{n}{gev\PYGZus{}params} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{estimator}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{maximize\PYGZus{}gev}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Estimated GEV Parameters:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{gev\PYGZus{}params}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
With these methods, you can estimate the parameters (shape, scale, location) of both the Fréchet and GEV distributions using the MLE approach.


\section{Step 5: Running Full Bootstrap for MLE}
\label{\detokenize{bootstrap:step-5-running-full-bootstrap-for-mle}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Finally, to estimate the variability of the MLE parameters, we can use the \sphinxtitleref{FullBootstrap} class. This class applies the full bootstrapping procedure, including resampling, block maxima extraction, and MLE estimation, to obtain mean and standard deviation estimates for the parameters.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to run the full bootstrap procedure for the Fréchet distribution:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{bootstrap} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{FullBootstrap}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example dataset}
\PYG{n}{sample} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{random}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{rand}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Initialize FullBootstrap with the dataset}
\PYG{n}{bootstrap} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{FullBootstrap}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{sample}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{10}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{dist\PYGZus{}type}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{Frechet}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Run the bootstrap procedure}
\PYG{n}{bootstrap}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{run\PYGZus{}bootstrap}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{num\PYGZus{}bootstraps}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Print the mean and standard deviation of the estimates}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Mean of Bootstrap Estimates:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bootstrap}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{statistics}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{mean}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Standard Deviation of Bootstrap Estimates:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bootstrap}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{statistics}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{std}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
This process generates multiple bootstrap samples, applies MLE to each, and calculates the mean and standard deviation of the resulting estimates. You can also do this for the GEV distribution by setting \sphinxtitleref{dist\_type=’GEV’}.


\section{Conclusion}
\label{\detokenize{bootstrap:conclusion}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this tutorial, we walked through the process of extracting block maxima, generating bootstrap samples, aggregating the data, and estimating parameters using MLE. We also saw how to apply the full bootstrap procedure to analyze the variability of the MLE estimates. This framework is essential when dealing with extreme value theory and understanding the uncertainty in parameter estimates.

\chapter{Bias Correction Tutorial}
\label{\detokenize{biascorrection:bias-correction-tutorial}}\label{\detokenize{biascorrection::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this tutorial, we will explore how to work with the \sphinxtitleref{topt} module from \sphinxtitleref{xtremes} to apply bias correction techniques in extreme value analysis. Specifically, we will cover:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Generating index ranges for sliding and disjoint block methods (\sphinxtitleref{I\_sb} and \sphinxtitleref{D\_n}).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Computing exceedances using \sphinxtitleref{exceedances}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Estimating cluster size probability using \sphinxtitleref{hat\_pi0}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Utilizing the \sphinxtitleref{Upsilon}, \sphinxtitleref{Pi}, and \sphinxtitleref{Psi} functions for bias correction.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Solving for bias\sphinxhyphen{}corrected parameters with \sphinxtitleref{varpi} and \sphinxtitleref{a1\_asy}.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Each section includes code examples for clarity.


\section{Step 1: Generating Block Indices}
\label{\detokenize{biascorrection:step-1-generating-block-indices}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In time series analysis, blocks of data are used to analyze cluster sizes and exceedances. We define two key functions:


\subsection{\sphinxtitleref{I\_sb(j, bs)}: Generate indices for a sliding block}
\label{\detokenize{biascorrection:i-sb-j-bs-generate-indices-for-a-sliding-block}}
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n+nn}{numpy} \PYG{k}{as} \PYG{n+nn}{np}

\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{I\PYGZus{}sb}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{j}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{arange}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{j}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{j} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example usage}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{I\PYGZus{}sb}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Output: [3, 4, 5, 6, 7]}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\subsection{\sphinxtitleref{D\_n(n, bs)}: Generate index pairs for disjoint blocks}
\label{\detokenize{biascorrection:d-n-n-bs-generate-index-pairs-for-disjoint-blocks}}
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{D\PYGZus{}n}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{n}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{n}{idx} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{arange}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{n} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}} \PYG{n}{bs} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{n}{pairs} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{p}{]}
    \PYG{k}{for} \PYG{n}{i} \PYG{o+ow}{in} \PYG{n}{idx}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{n}{left\PYGZus{}js} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{idx}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{idx} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{n}{bs} \PYG{o}{\PYGZlt{}}\PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{i}\PYG{p}{]}
        \PYG{n}{right\PYGZus{}js} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{idx}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{idx} \PYG{o}{\PYGZgt{}}\PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{i} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{]}
        \PYG{k}{for} \PYG{n}{j} \PYG{o+ow}{in} \PYG{n}{left\PYGZus{}js}\PYG{p}{:}
            \PYG{n}{pairs}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{append}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{i}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{j}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
        \PYG{k}{for} \PYG{n}{j} \PYG{o+ow}{in} \PYG{n}{right\PYGZus{}js}\PYG{p}{:}
            \PYG{n}{pairs}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{append}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{i}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{j}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{pairs}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example usage}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{D\PYGZus{}n}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Output: [(1, 3), (1, 4), (2, 4), (3, 1), (3, 2), (4, 1), (4, 2)]}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Step 2: Computing Exceedances}
\label{\detokenize{biascorrection:step-2-computing-exceedances}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxtitleref{exceedances} function counts values exceeding a given threshold in a block.

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{exceedances}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{i}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{j}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{k}{if} \PYG{n}{stride} \PYG{o}{==} \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{sum}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{j}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{o}{*}\PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{n}{j}\PYG{o}{*}\PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{]} \PYG{o}{\PYGZgt{}} \PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{i}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{k}{if} \PYG{n}{stride} \PYG{o}{==} \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{SBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{sum}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{j}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{n}{j}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{]} \PYG{o}{\PYGZgt{}} \PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{i}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example usage}
\PYG{n}{data} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{array}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{6}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{4}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{7}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{9}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{maxima} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{array}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{7}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{exceedances}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{i}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{j}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Output: 1}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Step 3: Estimating Cluster Size Probability}
\label{\detokenize{biascorrection:step-3-estimating-cluster-size-probability}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The function \sphinxtitleref{hat\_pi0} estimates the probability of cluster size 1.

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n+nn}{xtremes}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{topt} \PYG{k}{as} \PYG{n+nn}{topt}

\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{hat\PYGZus{}pi0}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{k+kc}{None}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{k+kc}{None}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{k}{if} \PYG{n}{maxima} \PYG{o+ow}{is} \PYG{o+ow}{not} \PYG{k+kc}{None}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{n}{bs} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n+nb}{len}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{/}\PYG{o}{/} \PYG{n+nb}{len}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{k}{elif} \PYG{n}{bs} \PYG{o+ow}{is} \PYG{o+ow}{not} \PYG{k+kc}{None}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{n}{maxima} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{topt}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{extract\PYGZus{}BM}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{k}{else}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{k}{raise} \PYG{n+ne}{ValueError}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{Either maxima or block size must be provided}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{n}{k} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n+nb}{len}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{k}{if} \PYG{n}{stride} \PYG{o}{==} \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{n}{s} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{sum}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{exceedances}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{i}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{j}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{==} \PYG{l+m+mi}{1}
                    \PYG{k}{for} \PYG{n}{i} \PYG{o+ow}{in} \PYG{n+nb}{range}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{1} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{n}{k}\PYG{p}{)}
                    \PYG{k}{for} \PYG{n}{j} \PYG{o+ow}{in} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{delete}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{arange}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{1} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{n}{k}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{i}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
        \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{l+m+mi}{4} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{n}{s} \PYG{o}{/} \PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{k} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{k}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example usage}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{hat\PYGZus{}pi0}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{data}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bs}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stride}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{DBM}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Step 4: Using Bias Correction Functions}
\label{\detokenize{biascorrection:step-4-using-bias-correction-functions}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
We define functions for the \sphinxtitleref{Upsilon}, \sphinxtitleref{Pi}, and \sphinxtitleref{Psi} functions used in bias correction.

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{scipy}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{special} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{digamma}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{polygamma}
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{scipy}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{optimize} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{root\PYGZus{}scalar}

\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{Upsilon}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{rho0} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{Upsilon\PYGZus{}derivative}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{rho0} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{n}{digamma}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{n}{digamma}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{Pi}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{o}{/}\PYG{n}{x} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}} \PYG{n}{Upsilon\PYGZus{}derivative}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{o}{/}\PYG{n}{Upsilon}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{o}{/}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{euler\PYGZus{}gamma}

\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{Psi}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{a}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{a\PYGZus{}true}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{n}{vp} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{a} \PYG{o}{/} \PYG{n}{a\PYGZus{}true}
    \PYG{n}{term} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+m+mi}{1} \PYG{o}{/} \PYG{n}{vp} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}} \PYG{n}{Upsilon\PYGZus{}derivative}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{vp}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{/} \PYG{n}{Upsilon}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{vp}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{o}{+} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{o}{/}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{euler\PYGZus{}gamma}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{l+m+mi}{2} \PYG{o}{/} \PYG{n}{a\PYGZus{}true} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{n}{term}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Step 5: Solving for Bias\sphinxhyphen{}Corrected Parameters}
\label{\detokenize{biascorrection:step-5-solving-for-bias-corrected-parameters}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
To obtain bias\sphinxhyphen{}corrected parameters, we use numerical root\sphinxhyphen{}finding methods.

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{varpi}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{n}{sol} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{root\PYGZus{}scalar}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{Pi}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{args}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bracket}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.01}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{10}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{sol}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{root}

\PYG{k}{def} \PYG{n+nf}{a1\PYGZus{}asy}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{a\PYGZus{}true}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{n}{sol} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{root\PYGZus{}scalar}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{Psi}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{args}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{a\PYGZus{}true}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{bracket}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mf}{1e\PYGZhy{}2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{sol}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{root}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example usage}
\PYG{n}{rho0} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{varpi}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{rho0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Computes bias\PYGZhy{}corrected root}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\chapter{Utility Functions for Extreme Value Analysis}
\label{\detokenize{miscellaneous:utility-functions-for-extreme-value-analysis}}\label{\detokenize{miscellaneous::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this tutorial, we will explore the utility functions provided in \sphinxtitleref{miscellaneous.py} that are used in the context of extreme value analysis. These functions include calculating probability\sphinxhyphen{}weighted moments, simulating time series, computing GEV distributions, and performing basic statistical tasks like sigmoid and inverse sigmoid transformations.

\sphinxAtStartPar
We will cover:
\sphinxhyphen{} Basic mathematical functions like \sphinxtitleref{sigmoid()} and \sphinxtitleref{mse()}.
\sphinxhyphen{} Functions related to Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distributions such as \sphinxtitleref{GEV\_pdf()}, \sphinxtitleref{GEV\_cdf()}, and \sphinxtitleref{PWM\_estimation()}.
\sphinxhyphen{} Simulating time series data using the \sphinxtitleref{simulate\_timeseries()} function.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Let’s go through each function step by step.


\section{Step 1: Using the Sigmoid and Inverse Sigmoid Functions}
\label{\detokenize{miscellaneous:step-1-using-the-sigmoid-and-inverse-sigmoid-functions}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The sigmoid function is used to map real\sphinxhyphen{}valued numbers into the range (0, 1), and the inverse sigmoid performs the opposite transformation.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how you can use the \sphinxtitleref{sigmoid()} and \sphinxtitleref{invsigmoid()} functions:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{miscellaneous} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{sigmoid}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{invsigmoid}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Apply sigmoid transformation}
\PYG{n}{x} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{]}
\PYG{n}{sigmoid\PYGZus{}values} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{sigmoid}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{sigmoid\PYGZus{}values}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Apply inverse sigmoid transformation}
\PYG{n}{y} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{0.9}\PYG{p}{]}
\PYG{n}{invsigmoid\PYGZus{}values} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{invsigmoid}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{y}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{invsigmoid\PYGZus{}values}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The sigmoid function maps any real\sphinxhyphen{}valued input to a value between 0 and 1, while the inverse sigmoid transforms probabilities back to their original values.


\section{Step 2: Calculating Probability Weighted Moments (PWM)}
\label{\detokenize{miscellaneous:step-2-calculating-probability-weighted-moments-pwm}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In extreme value theory, PWMs are used to estimate parameters of the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution. The function \sphinxtitleref{PWM\_estimation()} computes the first three PWMs based on block maxima.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to compute PWM for a set of block maxima:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{miscellaneous} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{PWM\PYGZus{}estimation}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example block maxima data}
\PYG{n}{maxima} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{8}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{12}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{15}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{18}\PYG{p}{]}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Compute PWM estimators}
\PYG{n}{beta\PYGZus{}0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{beta\PYGZus{}1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{beta\PYGZus{}2} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PWM\PYGZus{}estimation}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{maxima}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+sa}{f}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{β0: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{n}{beta\PYGZus{}0}\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{, β1: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{n}{beta\PYGZus{}1}\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{, β2: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{n}{beta\PYGZus{}2}\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
These PWMs can then be used to estimate GEV parameters using the \sphinxtitleref{PWM2GEV()} function, which converts PWMs to GEV parameters (shape, location, and scale).


\section{Step 3: Estimating GEV Parameters from PWM}
\label{\detokenize{miscellaneous:step-3-estimating-gev-parameters-from-pwm}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The function \sphinxtitleref{PWM2GEV()} converts the first three PWM moments into GEV distribution parameters: shape (γ), location (μ), and scale (σ).

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to compute GEV parameters from PWM estimators:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{miscellaneous} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{PWM2GEV}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} PWM estimators}
\PYG{n}{b\PYGZus{}0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{b\PYGZus{}1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{b\PYGZus{}2} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+m+mf}{11.6}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{11.2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mf}{39.2}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Compute GEV parameters}
\PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{mu}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{sigma} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PWM2GEV}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{b\PYGZus{}0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{b\PYGZus{}1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{b\PYGZus{}2}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+sa}{f}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{GEV Shape (γ): }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{, Location (μ): }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{n}{mu}\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{, Scale (σ): }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{n}{sigma}\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxtitleref{PWM2GEV()} function allows you to estimate the GEV distribution parameters based on the computed PWM moments.


\section{Step 4: Working with the GEV Distribution}
\label{\detokenize{miscellaneous:step-4-working-with-the-gev-distribution}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The module provides several functions to compute properties of the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution, including:
\sphinxhyphen{} \sphinxtitleref{GEV\_pdf()}: Computes the Probability Density Function (PDF).
\sphinxhyphen{} \sphinxtitleref{GEV\_cdf()}: Computes the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF).
\sphinxhyphen{} \sphinxtitleref{GEV\_ll()}: Computes the log\sphinxhyphen{}likelihood of the GEV distribution.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to use these functions:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{miscellaneous} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{GEV\PYGZus{}pdf}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{GEV\PYGZus{}cdf}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{GEV\PYGZus{}ll}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Example data}
\PYG{n}{x} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{4}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{]}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Compute GEV PDF}
\PYG{n}{pdf\PYGZus{}values} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{GEV\PYGZus{}pdf}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{mu}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{sigma}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{GEV PDF:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{pdf\PYGZus{}values}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Compute GEV CDF}
\PYG{n}{cdf\PYGZus{}values} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{GEV\PYGZus{}cdf}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{mu}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{sigma}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{GEV CDF:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{cdf\PYGZus{}values}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Compute GEV log\PYGZhy{}likelihood}
\PYG{n}{ll\PYGZus{}values} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{GEV\PYGZus{}ll}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{gamma}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{mu}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{2}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{sigma}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{GEV Log\PYGZhy{}Likelihood:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{ll\PYGZus{}values}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
These functions allow you to work with GEV distributions for various tasks like computing probabilities, densities, or performing likelihood\sphinxhyphen{}based inference.


\section{Step 5: Simulating Time Series Data}
\label{\detokenize{miscellaneous:step-5-simulating-time-series-data}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxtitleref{simulate\_timeseries()} function is a powerful utility to generate time series data with different distributions and correlation structures. You can simulate IID (independent and identically distributed) data or time series with temporal dependence using ARMAX or AR models.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Here’s how to simulate a time series:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{miscellaneous} \PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{simulate\PYGZus{}timeseries}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Simulate an IID time series with GEV distribution}
\PYG{n}{simulated\PYGZus{}ts} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{simulate\PYGZus{}timeseries}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{n}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{distr}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{GEV}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{correlation}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{IID}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{modelparams}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.5}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{seed}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{42}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Print the first 10 values}
\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{simulated\PYGZus{}ts}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{l+m+mi}{10}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
This function supports various distributions (e.g., GEV, Frechet, GPD) and allows you to introduce temporal dependence using ARMAX or AR models.


\section{Conclusion}
\label{\detokenize{miscellaneous:conclusion}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this tutorial, we explored several utility functions provided in \sphinxtitleref{miscellaneous.py} for extreme value analysis. These functions help in tasks ranging from basic mathematical transformations (like sigmoid) to more advanced operations like PWM estimation, GEV parameter estimation, and time series simulation.

\chapter{Reference: topt}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:reference-topt}}\label{\detokenize{api_topt::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This module is specialized in implementing Top\sphinxhyphen{}t based Maximum Likelihood Estimators.


\section{Overview}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:overview}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxtitleref{xtremes.topt} module provides tools for analyzing higher order statistics and their influence on Maximum Likelihood estimations. It includes classes and functions for handling time series data, extracting block maxima, and performing statistical analysis.


\section{Classes}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:classes}}

\section{The \sphinxstyleliteralintitle{\sphinxupquote{TimeSeries}} Class and its Functionalities}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:the-timeseries-class-and-its-functionalities}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxtitleref{TimeSeries} class is used to handle and manipulate time series data. It provides methods for extracting block maxima and high order statistics.
\index{TimeSeries (class in xtremes.topt)@\spxentry{TimeSeries}\spxextra{class in xtremes.topt}}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{api_topt:xtremes.topt.TimeSeries}}
\pysigstartsignatures
\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxbfcode{\sphinxupquote{class\DUrole{w,w}{  }}}\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{xtremes.topt.}}\sphinxbfcode{\sphinxupquote{TimeSeries}}}{\sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{n}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{distr}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{'GEV'}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{correlation}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{'IID'}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{modelparams}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{{[}0{]}}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{ts}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{0}}}{}

Bases: \sphinxhref{https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html\#object}{\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{object}}}

\sphinxAtStartPar
TimeSeries class for simulating and analyzing time series data with optional correlation structures.

\sphinxAtStartPar
This class is designed to simulate time series data based on specified distributions and correlation types. 
It also provides methods to extract block maxima and high order statistics from the simulated data.


\subsection{Parameters}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:parameters}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{n}{[}int{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The length of the time series.

\sphinxlineitem{distr}{[}str, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The distribution to simulate the time series data from. Default is ‘GEV’ (Generalized Extreme Value).

\sphinxlineitem{correlation}{[}str, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The type of correlation structure. Options include {[}‘IID’, ‘ARMAX’, ‘AR’{]}. Default is ‘IID’ 
(independent and identically distributed).

\sphinxlineitem{modelparams}{[}list, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The parameters of the specified distribution. Default is {[}0{]}.

\sphinxlineitem{ts}{[}float, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A parameter for controlling the time series characteristics, particularly for correlated series (e.g., in AR models).
Must be in the range {[}0, 1{]}. Default is 0.

\end{description}


\subsection{Attributes}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:attributes}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{values}{[}list{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A list to store the simulated time series data.

\sphinxlineitem{distr}{[}str{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The type of distribution used for generating the time series data.

\sphinxlineitem{corr}{[}str{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The type of correlation structure applied to the time series.

\sphinxlineitem{modelparams}{[}list{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The parameters of the chosen distribution model.

\sphinxlineitem{ts}{[}float{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A parameter controlling the correlation or time series structure, if applicable.

\sphinxlineitem{len}{[}int{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The length of the time series.

\sphinxlineitem{blockmaxima}{[}list{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A list storing block maxima extracted from the simulated data.

\sphinxlineitem{high\_order\_stats}{[}list{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A list storing the high order statistics extracted from the simulated data.

\end{description}


\subsection{Methods}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:methods}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{simulate(rep=10, seeds=’default’):}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Simulates time series data based on the given distribution and correlation type.

\sphinxlineitem{get\_blockmaxima(block\_size=2, stride=’DBM’, rep=10):}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Extracts block maxima from the simulated time series data.

\sphinxlineitem{get\_HOS(orderstats=2, block\_size=2, stride=’DBM’, rep=10):}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Extracts high order statistics from the simulated time series data.

\end{description}

\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Simulate time series data with 5 repetitions and specific seeds}

\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Extract block maxima using a block size of 5}

\index{get\_HOS() (xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method)@\spxentry{get\_HOS()}\spxextra{xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method}}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{api_topt:xtremes.topt.TimeSeries.get_HOS}}
\pysigstartsignatures
\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxbfcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_HOS}}}{\sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{orderstats}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{2}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{block\_size}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{2}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{stride}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{'DBM'}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{rep}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{10}}}{}
\pysigstopsignatures
\sphinxAtStartPar
Extract high order statistics from simulated time series data.

\sphinxAtStartPar
High order statistics include values such as the second\sphinxhyphen{}largest value within each block of the time series.


\subsubsection{Parameters}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:id1}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{orderstats}{[}int, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The order of statistics to extract. Default is 2 (i.e., second\sphinxhyphen{}highest value).

\sphinxlineitem{block\_size}{[}int, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The size of blocks from which to extract the statistics. Default is 2.

\sphinxlineitem{stride}{[}str, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The stride or step type used for block extraction. Choose from {[}‘SBM’, ‘DBM’{]}. Default is ‘DBM’.

\sphinxlineitem{rep}{[}int, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The number of repetitions for extracting high order statistics. Should match the number of simulations. Default is 10.

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{get\_blockmaxima() (xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method)@\spxentry{get\_blockmaxima()}\spxextra{xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method}}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{api_topt:xtremes.topt.TimeSeries.get_blockmaxima}}
\pysigstartsignatures
\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxbfcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_blockmaxima}}}{\sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{block\_size}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{2}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{stride}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{'DBM'}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{rep}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{10}}}{}
\pysigstopsignatures
\sphinxAtStartPar
Extract block maxima from simulated time series data.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Block maxima are the maximum values extracted from blocks of the time series data.


\subsubsection{Parameters}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:id2}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{block\_size}{[}int, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The size of blocks from which to extract maxima. Default is 2.

\sphinxlineitem{stride}{[}\{str, int\}, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The stride or step type used for block extraction. Choose from {[}‘SBM’ (Sliding Block Maxima), ‘DBM’ 
(Disjoint Block Maxima){]} or specify an integer as a step size. Default is ‘DBM’.

\sphinxlineitem{rep}{[}int, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The number of repetitions for maxima extraction. This should match the number of simulations. Default is 10.

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{plot() (xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method)@\spxentry{plot()}\spxextra{xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method}}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{api_topt:xtremes.topt.TimeSeries.plot}}
\pysigstartsignatures
\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxbfcode{\sphinxupquote{plot}}}{\sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{rep}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{1}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{filename}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{None}}}{}
\pysigstopsignatures
\sphinxAtStartPar
Plot the simulated time series data.

\sphinxAtStartPar
This method generates a plot showing the simulated time series data. The user can choose to display 
data from specific repetitions or all repetitions.


\subsubsection{Parameters}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:id3}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{rep}{[}int or list, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The repetition number(s) to plot. If 0, all repetitions are plotted. If a list is provided, 
only the specified repetitions are plotted. Default is 1.

\sphinxlineitem{filename}{[}str, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The name of the PNG file to save the plot. If None, the plot is displayed but not saved. Default is None.

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{plot\_blockmaxima() (xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method)@\spxentry{plot\_blockmaxima()}\spxextra{xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method}}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{api_topt:xtremes.topt.TimeSeries.plot_blockmaxima}}
\pysigstartsignatures
\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxbfcode{\sphinxupquote{plot\_blockmaxima}}}{\sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{rep}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{1}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{filename}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{None}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{plot\_type}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{'line'}}}{}
\pysigstopsignatures
\sphinxAtStartPar
Plot the extracted block maxima.

\sphinxAtStartPar
This method generates a plot showing the extracted block maxima from the simulated time series data. 
The user can choose to display block maxima from specific repetitions or all repetitions. The plot 
can be either a line plot or a histogram.


\subsubsection{Parameters}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:id4}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{rep}{[}int or list, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The repetition number(s) to plot. If 0, all repetitions are plotted. If a list is provided, 
only the specified repetitions are plotted. Default is 1.

\sphinxlineitem{filename}{[}str, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The name of the PNG file to save the plot. If None, the plot is displayed but not saved. Default is None.

\sphinxlineitem{plot\_type}{[}str, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The type of plot to generate. Options are ‘line’ for a line plot and ‘hist’ for a histogram. Default is ‘line’.

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{plot\_with\_blockmaxima() (xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method)@\spxentry{plot\_with\_blockmaxima()}\spxextra{xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method}}

\begin{fulllineitems}

\pysigstartsignatures

\pysigstopsignatures
\sphinxAtStartPar
Plot the simulated time series data along with block maxima.

\sphinxAtStartPar
This method generates a plot showing the simulated time series data along with the block maxima. 
The user can choose to display data from specific repetitions or all repetitions.


\subsubsection{Parameters}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:id5}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{rep}{[}int, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The repetition number to plot. Default is 1.

\sphinxlineitem{plotlim}{[}int, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The limit for the number of data points to plot. Default is 300.

\sphinxlineitem{filename}{[}str, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The name of the PNG file to save the plot. If None, the plot is displayed but not saved. Default is None.

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{simulate() (xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method)@\spxentry{simulate()}\spxextra{xtremes.topt.TimeSeries method}}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{api_topt:xtremes.topt.TimeSeries.simulate}}
\pysigstartsignatures
\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxbfcode{\sphinxupquote{simulate}}}{\sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{rep}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{10}}\sphinxparamcomma \sphinxparam{\DUrole{n,n}{seeds}\DUrole{o,o}{=}\DUrole{default_value}{'default'}}}{}
\pysigstopsignatures
\sphinxAtStartPar
Simulate time series data.

\sphinxAtStartPar
This method generates time series data based on the specified distribution, correlation type, and other parameters.


\subsubsection{Parameters}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:id6}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{rep}{[}int, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The number of repetitions (simulations) to run. Default is 10.

\sphinxlineitem{seeds}{[}\{str, list\}, optional{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Seed(s) for random number generation. If ‘default’, a sequence of seeds is automatically generated 
based on the number of repetitions. If a list of seeds is provided, it must match the number of 
repetitions (\sphinxtitleref{rep}). Default is ‘default’.

\end{description}


\subsubsection{Raises}
\label{\detokenize{api_topt:raises}}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{ValueError}
\sphinxAtStartPar
If the number of provided seeds does not match the number of repetitions.

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}


\end{fulllineitems}
